questions.title: BFS and DFS properties
questions.prompt: State two key properties of Breadth-First Search (BFS) and two of Depth-First Search (DFS). For each algorithm, give its time complexity on a graph G = (V, E) represented as an adjacency list.

markscheme.text: BFS: explores vertices in non-decreasing order of distance from source; produces a BFS tree where shortest paths (by edge count) to all reachable vertices are encoded
DFS: explores as far as possible before backtracking; classifies edges as tree/back/forward/cross; completes a connected component before moving on
Both run in O(V + E) on an adjacency list (each vertex and edge visited once)

questions.title: Dijkstra's algorithm correctness
questions.prompt: Explain why Dijkstra's single-source shortest-path algorithm requires all edge weights to be non-negative. Give a small counterexample showing it can fail with a negative edge.

markscheme.text: Dijkstra's greedy invariant: once a vertex u is extracted from the priority queue with distance d[u], that distance is assumed final. A negative edge from a later-processed vertex could provide a shorter path to u, violating this assumption
Counterexample: graph with edges A→B weight 2, A→C weight 3, C→B weight −2. Dijkstra extracts B first (dist=2), marks it settled; but path A→C→B has weight 1 — shorter, but missed

questions.title: Running Dijkstra's algorithm
questions.prompt:
Run Dijkstra's algorithm on the following graph from source A. Record the distance estimate and predecessor for each vertex at each step.
Edges (directed, with weights): A→B:4, A→C:2, B→C:1, B→D:5, C→B:1, C→D:8, C→E:10, D→E:2, E→D:6. All weights are positive.
questions.hint: At each step, extract the unvisited vertex with minimum tentative distance and relax its outgoing edges.

markscheme.text: Initial: dist[A]=0, all others ∞. Extract A: relax B→4, C→2
Extract C (dist=2): relax B→min(4,3)=3, D→10, E→12
Extract B (dist=3): relax D→min(10,8)=8
Extract D (dist=8): relax E→min(12,10)=10
Extract E (dist=10). Final distances: A=0, B=3, C=2, D=8, E=10

questions.title: Bellman-Ford and negative cycles
questions.prompt:
(i) Describe the Bellman-Ford algorithm and give its time complexity.
(ii) Explain how Bellman-Ford detects negative-weight cycles.
(iii) Give a directed graph with 4 nodes that contains a negative cycle, and show what Bellman-Ford reports.
questions.hint: After V−1 relaxations, if any edge can still be relaxed, a negative cycle exists.

markscheme.text: Bellman-Ford: initialise dist[s]=0, rest ∞. Repeat V−1 times: for all edges (u,v,w), if dist[u]+w < dist[v], update dist[v]. Time: O(VE)
Negative cycle detection: perform one additional (V-th) round of relaxations; if any dist[v] decreases, a negative cycle is reachable from s

Example: nodes A,B,C,D. Edges A→B:1, B→C:−3, C→D:2, D→B:1. Cycle B→C→D→B has weight −3+2+1=0? No, try B→C:−3, C→B:1 → cycle weight −2. Bellman-Ford: after 3 rounds, dist[C] keeps decreasing → negative cycle detected on round 4

questions.title: Shortest paths: algorithm selection and analysis
questions.prompt: This question concerns shortest-path algorithms.

parts.label: a
parts.prompt:
For each of the following scenarios, state which shortest-path algorithm you would use and justify your choice:
(i) Dense graph, non-negative weights, single source.
(ii) Sparse graph, non-negative weights, single source.
(iii) Graph may contain negative edges (but no negative cycles), single source.
(iv) All-pairs shortest paths, dense graph.

markscheme.text: (i) Dijkstra with adjacency matrix + simple array priority queue: O(V²), suitable for dense graphs
(ii) Dijkstra with binary heap/Fibonacci heap + adjacency list: O((V+E)log V) or O(E + V log V)
(iii) Bellman-Ford: O(VE); handles negative edges, detects negative cycles
(iv) Floyd-Warshall: O(V³) DP; works well for dense graphs; or run Dijkstra V times for sparse graphs

parts.label: b
parts.prompt: Describe the Floyd-Warshall algorithm for all-pairs shortest paths. Write the recurrence relation and give the time and space complexity.

markscheme.text: DP state: dist[i][j][k] = shortest path from i to j using only vertices {1,...,k} as intermediates
Recurrence: dist[i][j][k] = min(dist[i][j][k−1], dist[i][k][k−1] + dist[k][j][k−1])
Base case: dist[i][j][0] = w(i,j) if edge exists, 0 if i=j, ∞ otherwise. Final answer in dist[i][j][V]
Time O(V³), space O(V²) (only need two layers at a time, or optimise to O(V²) by updating in-place)

parts.label: c
parts.prompt: Prove that BFS finds shortest paths (by number of edges) in an unweighted graph. Your proof should use a loop invariant.

markscheme.text: Invariant: when a vertex v is added to the queue at distance d, dist[v] = d is the true shortest-path distance from s

Induction: dist[s]=0 trivially correct. For vertex v extracted at distance d: v was enqueued when processing some u with dist[u]=d−1; edge (u,v) gives dist[v]≤d. Any shorter path would have enqueued v earlier at distance < d, contradiction (FIFO property of BFS queue)
Conclusion: all vertices at distance d are enqueued before any at distance d+1 (by induction and FIFO), so BFS processes vertices in non-decreasing distance order

parts.label: d
parts.prompt:
Dijkstra's algorithm can be implemented with different priority queue data structures. Complete the table: for each PQ implementation, give the time for decreaseKey and extractMin, and state the overall complexity of Dijkstra on G=(V,E).
PQ type: (i) unsorted array, (ii) binary heap, (iii) Fibonacci heap.

markscheme.text: (i) Unsorted array: extractMin O(V), decreaseKey O(1); Dijkstra total O(V²) — good for dense graphs
(ii) Binary heap: extractMin O(log V), decreaseKey O(log V); Dijkstra total O((V+E) log V)
(iii) Fibonacci heap: extractMin O(log V) amortised, decreaseKey O(1) amortised; Dijkstra total O(E + V log V) — theoretically optimal for sparse graphs

questions.title: DFS and topological sort
questions.prompt: This question concerns Depth-First Search and its applications.

parts.label: a
parts.prompt: Describe the DFS algorithm on a directed graph G=(V,E) and explain how finish times are assigned. How are edge types (tree, back, forward, cross) characterised in terms of DFS timestamps?

markscheme.text: DFS: maintain discover[v] (grey) and finish[v] (black) timestamps. Recurse on all unvisited neighbours before finishing v
Tree edge: v discovered via edge (u,v); Back edge: (u,v) where v is an ancestor of u (v is grey when (u,v) explored)

Forward edge: (u,v) where v is a descendant of u but not direct tree child (v is black, discover[u] < discover[v]); Cross edge: (u,v) where v is in a different DFS tree (v is black, discover[u] > discover[v])

parts.label: b
parts.prompt: Prove that a directed graph has a topological ordering if and only if it is a DAG (directed acyclic graph).

markscheme.text: (→) If a topological ordering exists, a cycle would require some vertex to come before itself — contradiction. So the graph must be a DAG
(←) If G is a DAG: run DFS. Claim: outputting vertices in reverse finish order gives a topological ordering

Proof of claim: for any edge (u,v) in a DAG, finish[v] < finish[u] (v finishes before u since DFS fully explores v before returning to u). DAGs have no back edges. So reverse finish order is a valid topological order

parts.label: c
parts.prompt: Run DFS on the DAG with vertices {A,B,C,D,E,F} and edges {A→C, A→B, B→D, C→D, C→E, D→F, E→F}. Starting from A, list the finish times and give the topological order.

markscheme.text: Valid DFS traversal (order may vary): A→C→D→F(finish F)→(back to D, finish D)→(back to C)→E→(F already visited, finish E)→(finish C)→B→(D visited, finish B)→(finish A)
Finish order (one valid run): F, D, E, C, B, A (or similar consistent with DFS)
Topological order (reverse finish): A, B, C, E, D, F (or any valid topological sort consistent with the DFS run)

parts.label: d
parts.prompt: Describe Kosaraju's algorithm for finding strongly connected components (SCCs). State its time complexity.

markscheme.text: Step 1: Run DFS on G; record finish times for all vertices
Step 2: Compute G^T (transpose graph — reverse all edges)
Step 3: Run DFS on G^T in decreasing finish-time order; each DFS tree is an SCC. Time: O(V+E)

questions.title: Spanning tree definitions
questions.prompt: Define: (i) a spanning tree of an undirected connected graph G; (ii) a minimum spanning tree (MST). State how many edges an MST of a graph with n vertices has.

markscheme.text: Spanning tree: a subgraph of G that is a tree and includes all vertices of G (connected, acyclic, V vertices)
MST: a spanning tree whose total edge weight is minimised over all spanning trees of G
An MST of a graph with n vertices has exactly n−1 edges

questions.title: Cut property for MSTs
questions.prompt: State the cut property for minimum spanning trees. Explain informally why it is true. How does this property justify Kruskal's algorithm?

markscheme.text: Cut property: for any cut (S, V\S) of a connected graph with distinct edge weights, the minimum-weight edge crossing the cut is in every MST

Justification: suppose the min-cut edge e = (u,v) is not in some MST T. Adding e to T creates a cycle; at least one other edge e' crosses the cut (since u and v are on opposite sides). Since w(e) < w(e'), swapping e' for e gives a lighter spanning tree — contradiction
Kruskal's: processes edges in order of increasing weight; adds edge (u,v) if it connects two different components — exactly the minimum-weight edge crossing the cut between those components

questions.title: Running Kruskal's algorithm
questions.prompt: Apply Kruskal's algorithm to find an MST of the graph with vertices {A,B,C,D,E} and edges: A-B:1, A-C:3, B-C:2, B-D:4, C-D:5, C-E:6, D-E:2. Show the Union-Find structure after each edge addition.
questions.hint: Sort edges by weight, then add an edge if its endpoints are in different components. Track components with Union-Find.

markscheme.text: Sort edges: A-B:1, B-C:2, D-E:2, A-C:3, B-D:4, C-D:5, C-E:6
Add A-B:1 (components: {A,B},{C},{D},{E}); Add B-C:2 → {A,B,C},{D},{E}; Add D-E:2 → {A,B,C},{D,E}
Skip A-C:3 (A and C already connected); Add B-D:4 → {A,B,C,D,E} — MST complete (4 edges for 5 vertices)
MST edges: A-B:1, B-C:2, D-E:2, B-D:4; total weight = 9

questions.title: Kruskal vs Prim algorithm comparison
questions.prompt:
(i) Describe Prim's MST algorithm and give its time complexity with a binary heap priority queue.
(ii) Compare Prim's and Kruskal's algorithms: which is preferable for sparse graphs? For dense graphs? Explain why.
(iii) State and prove the cycle property for MSTs.
questions.hint: The cycle property is the 'dual' of the cut property.

markscheme.text: Prim: grow MST greedily from a starting vertex. Maintain a priority queue of non-tree vertices keyed by minimum edge weight to the current tree. Repeatedly extract minimum and add to tree. O((V+E) log V) with binary heap

Sparse graphs (E ≈ V): Kruskal's O(E log E) = O(E log V) — sorting dominates, small E is fast. Prim with Fibonacci heap O(E + V log V) is also good. Kruskal preferred for sparse graphs when implemented with efficient Union-Find
Dense graphs (E ≈ V²): Prim with adjacency matrix runs in O(V²) which beats Kruskal's O(E log V) = O(V² log V)

Cycle property: for any cycle in G, the maximum-weight edge in that cycle is not in any MST. Proof: if max edge e were in MST T, removing e splits T into two components; re-adding any other edge of the cycle (weight < w(e)) reconnects them with lower total weight — contradiction

questions.title: Graph connectivity and network flow
questions.prompt: This question concerns graph connectivity.

parts.label: a
parts.prompt: Define (i) a bridge and (ii) an articulation point (cut vertex) in an undirected graph. Give an example graph containing both.

markscheme.text: Bridge: an edge whose removal disconnects the graph (increases number of connected components)
Articulation point: a vertex whose removal disconnects the graph. Example: path graph A-B-C: B is an articulation point, A-B and B-C are bridges

parts.label: b
parts.prompt: Describe how DFS can be used to find all bridges in an undirected graph in O(V+E) time. Define the 'low' value used in the algorithm.

markscheme.text: DFS tree: assign discovery times disc[v] to each vertex when first visited
low[v] = min of: disc[v], disc[w] for all back edges (v,w), low[u] for all tree edges (v,u)
An edge (u,v) (where v is a child of u in DFS tree) is a bridge if and only if low[v] > disc[u] — i.e., no back edge from v's subtree reaches u or an ancestor of u
Single DFS pass computes all disc[] and low[] values, checking bridge condition for each tree edge: O(V+E)

parts.label: c
parts.prompt: Define the maximum flow problem. State the max-flow min-cut theorem. Give an informal justification.

markscheme.text: Max flow: given directed graph G=(V,E) with capacities c(u,v), source s and sink t, find a flow f (satisfying capacity and conservation constraints) that maximises flow from s to t
Max-flow min-cut theorem: maximum value of a flow = minimum capacity of an s-t cut (a partition (S,T) of V with s∈S, t∈T; cut capacity = sum of capacities of edges from S to T)

Informal justification: every unit of flow from s to t must cross some s-t cut; a flow cannot exceed the min-cut capacity. Conversely, the Ford-Fulkerson algorithm achieves flow = min-cut (augmenting paths until residual graph has no s-t path)

parts.label: d
parts.prompt: State the Ford-Fulkerson algorithm. Give its time complexity if augmenting paths are found by BFS (Edmonds-Karp). Why does using DFS to find augmenting paths give a worse bound?

markscheme.text: Ford-Fulkerson: while there exists an augmenting path in the residual graph, push flow along it and update residual capacities. Repeat until no augmenting path exists
Edmonds-Karp (BFS): O(VE²) — each BFS finds a shortest augmenting path; at most O(VE) augmentations each taking O(E)
DFS: no guarantee of short paths; with integer capacities, worst case O(E·f*) augmentations (where f* is max flow value) — can be exponential in the network description size

questions.title: Union-Find and MST correctness
questions.prompt: This question concerns the Union-Find (disjoint set) data structure.

parts.label: a
parts.prompt: Describe the Union-Find data structure with union-by-rank and path compression. State the amortised time per operation.

markscheme.text: Forest of trees; each set represented by a tree; each node stores parent and rank. Find(x): traverse to root (path compress: make all nodes on path point directly to root)
Union(x,y): find roots rx, ry; if rank(rx) < rank(ry), make rx point to ry (and vice versa); if equal, increment rank of new root
With both optimisations: amortised O(α(n)) per operation, where α is the inverse Ackermann function (practically constant)

parts.label: b
parts.prompt: Prove that Kruskal's algorithm produces a minimum spanning tree. Your proof should use the cut property.

markscheme.text: Kruskal's produces a spanning tree T. We show it is minimum by showing every edge it adds satisfies the cut property

When Kruskal's adds edge e=(u,v): at this point, u and v are in different components C_u and C_v. The cut (C_u, V\C_u) has e as its minimum-weight crossing edge (all lighter edges were either already added or created a cycle within a component)
By the cut property, e is in every MST. Since Kruskal's adds exactly n−1 such edges and produces a spanning tree, it produces an MST

parts.label: c
parts.prompt: Is the MST of a graph unique? State conditions under which it is guaranteed to be unique, and give a counterexample when those conditions fail.

markscheme.text: MST is unique if all edge weights are distinct (follows from the cut property: the unique minimum-weight edge crossing any cut must be in the MST)
Not unique with ties: graph A-B:1, A-C:1, B-C:1 has three spanning trees, any two edges form an MST

parts.label: d
parts.prompt: A graph G has an MST T. One edge weight is decreased by δ > 0. Describe an efficient algorithm to update the MST.

markscheme.text: Case 1: decreased edge (u,v) is already in T. Decreasing its weight keeps T valid as an MST (T's total weight only decreases, and T was optimal before)
Case 2: decreased edge (u,v) is not in T. Adding (u,v) to T creates a unique cycle. If the new weight of (u,v) is less than the maximum-weight edge e_max on the cycle, swap e_max for (u,v)
Efficiency: finding the cycle path between u and v in T takes O(V). Checking the maximum edge on that path and performing the swap takes O(V). Total: O(V)

questions.title: Trie definition and operations
questions.prompt: Define a trie (prefix tree). State the time complexity of insert, search, and startsWith (prefix query) operations on a trie storing n strings of maximum length L.

markscheme.text: Trie: a tree data structure where each path from root to a node spells out a prefix; children are labelled by characters of an alphabet Σ
Insert: O(L) — follow/create nodes for each character of the string
Search and startsWith: O(L) — traverse the path for the query string; for search, check the end-of-word flag; for prefix query, simply check the node exists

questions.title: Amortised analysis of Union-Find
questions.prompt: Explain what amortised O(α(n)) time complexity means for Union-Find operations. Why is the inverse Ackermann function α(n) considered 'practically constant'? What does path compression achieve structurally?

markscheme.text: Amortised O(α(n)): over a sequence of m operations, total cost is O(m·α(n)); any single operation may be slower but the average is O(α(n))
α(n) grows so slowly that α(n) ≤ 4 for all n ≤ 2^(2^(2^(2^65536))) — any conceivable real-world input
Path compression: when Find(x) traverses up to the root, all nodes on the path are made direct children of the root. Flattens the tree, making future finds faster
Union by rank: without path compression, ensures tree height ≤ log n; combined with path compression gives the nearly-linear total complexity

questions.title: Augmented BST: order statistics
questions.prompt: An order-statistics tree augments a red-black BST with a size field at each node (the number of nodes in that node's subtree). Describe how to implement Select(i) — return the i-th smallest element — and Rank(x) — return the rank of element x — in O(log n) time.
questions.hint: At each node, the rank of the node's key depends on the size of its left subtree.

markscheme.text: Select(i): at node v with left subtree size r. If i = r+1, return v. If i ≤ r, recurse into left child with Select(i). If i > r+1, recurse into right child with Select(i−r−1)
Rank(x): start at root, track rank = 0. If x = node key, rank += left-size + 1 and return. If x > node key, rank += left-size + 1, recurse right. If x < node key, recurse left
Both O(log n): each step descends one level in the tree; tree height is O(log n) for a balanced BST

questions.title: Red-black tree properties and rotation
questions.prompt:
(i) State all five red-black tree properties.
(ii) Explain why a red-black tree with n nodes has height at most 2⌊log₂(n+1)⌋.
(iii) Briefly describe what happens when a new node is inserted into a red-black tree (the rebalancing cases).
questions.hint: For (ii), count black nodes on any root-to-leaf path and use the property that the height of a 'black-only' path bounds the full height.

markscheme.text: Five properties: (1) every node is red or black; (2) root is black; (3) every leaf (NIL) is black; (4) both children of every red node are black; (5) all simple paths from any node to its descendant leaves have the same number of black nodes (black-height)

Height bound: let bh = black-height of root. By property 5, every path has bh black nodes; by property 4, no two consecutive red nodes → red nodes ≤ bh. So height h ≤ 2·bh. Minimum nodes in subtree of black-height bh is 2^bh − 1, so n ≥ 2^bh − 1 → bh ≤ log₂(n+1) → h ≤ 2log₂(n+1)

Insertion: new node inserted red. Cases: (1) parent is black → done; (2) parent is red, uncle is red → recolour parent+uncle black, grandparent red, recurse upward; (3) parent is red, uncle is black → rotations (LL/RR/LR/RL-style) to restore properties. At most O(log n) recolours, O(1) rotations

questions.title: B-trees and disk-based storage
questions.prompt: This question concerns B-trees, used in database systems and file systems.

parts.label: a
parts.prompt: Define a B-tree of minimum degree t. State the constraints on the number of keys and children for (i) non-root nodes and (ii) the root. What is the maximum height of a B-tree with n keys?

markscheme.text: B-tree min degree t: every non-root node has at least t−1 keys (and t children if internal); every node has at most 2t−1 keys (and 2t children if internal)
Root: at least 1 key (and 2 children if not a leaf); at most 2t−1 keys
Maximum height: h ≤ log_t((n+1)/2) = O(log_t n). For t=2 (2-3-4 tree), height is O(log n); large t means fewer disk accesses for the same n

parts.label: b
parts.prompt: Describe the B-tree search algorithm. Explain why large values of t are preferred in practice (e.g. t = 100 or more in a database index).

markscheme.text: B-tree search: at each node, binary search among keys k_1 < k_2 < ... < k_n. If target = k_i, found. If target < k_1, descend to child pointer c_1. Etc. Recurse until found or reach leaf

Large t: a node fills one or more disk blocks; reading a node costs one disk I/O. With t=100, a B-tree with 10^9 keys has height ≤ 3 (since 100^3 = 10^6 ... hmm). Actually t=1000: height ≤ log_{1000}(10^9) = 3. 3 disk reads for any lookup in a billion-key index
Key insight: disk access time (ms) dominates CPU time (ns); minimising tree height minimises disk I/Os; large branching factor gives shallow trees

parts.label: c
parts.prompt: Describe the B-tree insertion algorithm at a high level. Explain the 'split' operation — when does it occur, what does it do, and how does it maintain the B-tree invariants?

markscheme.text: Insertion: search for the correct leaf position. If leaf has room (<2t−1 keys), insert. Otherwise, split required
Split: when a node has 2t−1 keys (full), split it into two nodes of t−1 keys each; promote the median key to the parent

Invariant maintenance: each half-node has ≥ t−1 keys (minimum); promoting the median grows the parent (which may itself need splitting, propagating upward). If root splits, new root is created — this is the only way height increases

parts.label: d
parts.prompt: Compare B-trees with red-black trees as implementations of an ordered dictionary. In what setting is each preferable?

markscheme.text: Red-black tree: O(log n) for all operations; binary branching; all data in RAM; better constant factors for small n; used in main-memory data structures (e.g. std::map in C++)
B-tree: O(log_t n) height; high branching factor; each node fits one disk block; minimises disk I/Os; used for database indexes, file systems (NTFS, ext4) where data exceeds RAM

questions.title: Skip lists and probabilistic data structures
questions.prompt: This question concerns skip lists.

parts.label: a
parts.prompt: Describe the skip list data structure. How are the levels of each node determined? What is the expected height of a skip list with n elements?

markscheme.text: Skip list: a linked list with multiple levels. Level 1 contains all elements; each higher level contains a random subset. Elements are sorted at each level
Level promotion: each node independently promoted to next level with probability p (typically 1/2). A node at level i is promoted to i+1 with prob p
Expected height: O(log n) for p=1/2 — by geometric distribution, expected number of levels is log_{1/p}(n)

parts.label: b
parts.prompt: Describe the search algorithm for a skip list. What is the expected time complexity of search, insert, and delete?

markscheme.text: Search: start at top-left. At each position, advance right if next element ≤ target; drop to next level otherwise. Continue until found or level 1 exhausted
Expected O(log n) per operation: at each level, expected O(1/p) = O(1) steps before dropping; O(log n) levels total
Insert and delete: same navigation as search, then O(1) pointer updates per level; still expected O(log n) total

parts.label: c
parts.prompt: Compare skip lists with balanced BSTs (e.g. AVL trees). Give one advantage and one disadvantage of skip lists.

markscheme.text: Advantage: simpler to implement (no rotations); concurrent access easier (fine-grained locking per node rather than tree rotations); constant factors often better in practice
Disadvantage: expected O(log n) vs guaranteed O(log n) for balanced BSTs; higher space usage (O(n log n) expected due to multiple pointers per element vs O(n) for BST)

parts.label: d
parts.prompt: Bloom filters are another probabilistic data structure. Describe what a Bloom filter supports and state one key limitation.

markscheme.text: Bloom filter: space-efficient structure supporting insert and membership query. Uses a bit array of size m and k independent hash functions. Insert: set bits at all k positions. Query: check if all k bits are set
Limitation: false positives possible (a query may return 'present' for an element never inserted); false negatives are impossible. Also: deletions are not supported in the basic version

questions.title: Convex hull definition
questions.prompt: Define the convex hull of a set of points P in the plane. State the output format. Name two algorithms for computing the convex hull and give their time complexities.

markscheme.text: Convex hull: the smallest convex polygon containing all points in P; equivalently, the intersection of all half-planes containing P
Output: vertices of the convex polygon in (counter-)clockwise order
Graham scan: O(n log n); Jarvis march (gift wrapping): O(nh) where h is number of hull vertices; also acceptable: QuickHull O(n log n) expected

questions.title: Cross product for orientation tests
questions.prompt: Explain how the cross product of two 2D vectors is used to determine the orientation of three points P, Q, R (left turn, right turn, or collinear). Write the formula and explain what each sign means.

markscheme.text: Given three points P, Q, R: form vectors QP = P−Q and QR = R−Q (or equivalently compute the signed area of triangle PQR)
Cross product (z-component): (Q.x−P.x)(R.y−P.y) − (Q.y−P.y)(R.x−P.x)
Positive: left turn (counter-clockwise, P→Q→R is CCW); Negative: right turn (clockwise); Zero: P, Q, R are collinear

questions.title: Graham scan algorithm
questions.prompt: Describe Graham's scan algorithm for convex hull computation. Illustrate by running it on the points: (0,0), (1,3), (2,1), (3,4), (4,0), (2,2).
questions.hint: Sort by angle from the lowest point, then scan maintaining a stack where we remove points causing non-left-turns.

markscheme.text: Step 1: find the lowest point (break ties by x-coordinate): (0,0). Sort remaining points by polar angle with respect to (0,0)
Approximate polar order from (0,0): (4,0), (2,1), (2,2), (1,3), (3,4) — exact order depends on angles calculated
Graham scan: push first 3 sorted points. For each new point, while top two stack points + new point make a non-left turn (CW or collinear), pop. Push new point
Convex hull vertices: (0,0), (4,0), (3,4), (1,3) (or similar valid convex hull; must check each point correctly included/excluded)

questions.title: Closest pair of points
questions.prompt:
(i) Describe the divide-and-conquer algorithm for closest pair of points. State the recurrence and resulting complexity.
(ii) Explain the key insight used in the combine step: why is it sufficient to check only O(1) candidate pairs per point in the strip?
questions.hint: In the strip of width 2δ around the dividing line, how many points can fit in a 2δ × δ rectangle while being at distance > δ from each other?

markscheme.text: Algorithm: sort by x. Divide at median x-coordinate. Recursively find δ = min(closest pair in left, closest pair in right). Combine: check pairs (p,q) with |p.x − median| < δ and |p.y − q.y| < δ (the strip)
Recurrence: T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n log n) [strip sorting each time] → T(n)=O(n log² n); or presort by y: T(n)=2T(n/2)+O(n) → T(n)=O(n log n)

Combine step insight: in the strip, all points are > δ apart. A 2δ × δ rectangle (δ in the x-direction around the line, δ in the y-direction) can contain at most 8 points (pack at distance δ: 2 columns × 4 rows of δ/2 grid). So each point in the strip has at most 7 (constant) neighbours to check

questions.title: Line segment intersection and sweep line
questions.prompt: This question concerns geometric algorithms using the sweep line technique.

parts.label: a
parts.prompt: Describe the problem of detecting whether any two line segments in a set S of n segments intersect. State the naïve O(n²) approach and explain why the Shamos-Hoey algorithm achieves O(n log n).

markscheme.text: Naïve: test all O(n²) pairs of segments for intersection; each test is O(1) using cross product
Shamos-Hoey: sweep a vertical line from left to right. Events: segment endpoints (n left and n right endpoints = 2n events). Maintain ordered status structure of currently active segments

Key observation: if two segments intersect, they must be adjacent in the status structure at some point during the sweep (just before or at intersection). Check only adjacent pairs → O(n log n) total with O(n log n) event processing

parts.label: b
parts.prompt: Describe the Bentley-Ottmann sweep line algorithm that reports all k intersections in O((n + k) log n) time. What data structures does it use and what events does it handle?

markscheme.text: Event queue: priority queue of events ordered by x-coordinate: (1) left endpoint of segment, (2) right endpoint, (3) intersection points discovered during sweep
Status structure: balanced BST of segments ordered by y-coordinate at the current sweep line position
Left endpoint: insert segment into status; check against its new neighbours for intersections; add intersection events to queue
Right endpoint: remove segment; check its former neighbours against each other. Intersection event: swap two segments in status; check each against their new neighbours. Total: O((n+k) log n)

parts.label: c
parts.prompt: Describe the point-in-polygon test using ray casting. What special cases must be handled?

markscheme.text: Ray casting: cast a ray from the query point q in any direction (e.g. rightward). Count the number of polygon edges intersected by the ray. Odd count = inside; even count = outside
Special case 1: ray passes exactly through a vertex — count it as half an intersection (or perturb the ray direction slightly)

Special case 2: ray is collinear with an edge — can cause double or zero counting; handle by only counting edges where one endpoint is strictly above and one below the ray, counting vertices on the ray consistently

parts.label: d
parts.prompt: What is the time complexity of the point-in-polygon test for a polygon with n vertices? Describe an improved data structure for repeated point-in-polygon queries on the same polygon.

markscheme.text: Single query: O(n) — check ray against each of n edges
Repeated queries: preprocess polygon into a trapezoidal decomposition or slab decomposition in O(n log n); each query then O(log n) using binary search

questions.title: Voronoi diagrams and Delaunay triangulations
questions.prompt: This question concerns Voronoi diagrams.

parts.label: a
parts.prompt: Define the Voronoi diagram of a set P of n point sites in the plane. How many vertices, edges, and faces does a Voronoi diagram of n sites have (in general position)?

markscheme.text: Voronoi diagram: partition of the plane into n regions, one per site p_i; the region V(p_i) = all points closer to p_i than to any other site
By Euler's formula: n faces, 2n−5 vertices, 3n−6 edges (for n ≥ 3 in general position)

parts.label: b
parts.prompt: State Fortune's algorithm for constructing the Voronoi diagram. What is its time complexity? What technique does it use?

markscheme.text: Fortune's algorithm: sweep line technique with a parabolic front (beach line). Event types: site events (new parabola added) and circle events (three beach line arcs converge — Voronoi vertex created)
Time: O(n log n) with O(n) space; asymptotically optimal (matches Ω(n log n) lower bound for sorting, which reduces to Voronoi)

parts.label: c
parts.prompt: Define the Delaunay triangulation of a point set P. State the relationship between the Delaunay triangulation and the Voronoi diagram. State the empty circumcircle property.

markscheme.text: Delaunay triangulation: a triangulation of P such that no point of P lies inside the circumcircle of any triangle (empty circumcircle property)
Relationship: the Delaunay triangulation is the dual graph of the Voronoi diagram — connect two sites p_i, p_j iff their Voronoi cells share an edge
Empty circumcircle: for every triangle pqr in the Delaunay triangulation, the circumcircle of pqr contains no other point of P in its interior

parts.label: d
parts.prompt: Give two practical applications of Voronoi diagrams and one application of Delaunay triangulations.

markscheme.text: Voronoi applications (2 of): nearest-neighbour search; facility location (find empty Voronoi vertex to place new facility maximally far from existing ones); computational biology (protein structure); geographic analysis (catchment areas)
Delaunay application: mesh generation for finite element analysis (maximises the minimum angle of triangles, avoiding sliver triangles); also: terrain modelling, interpolation
